GS2.headline: Parliament Passes Jan Vishwas (Amendment) Bill, Decriminalizing Minor Offences
GS2.explainer:
• The Parliament successfully passed the Jan Vishwas (Amendment of Provisions) Bill, 2025, which aims to decriminalize minor offences across various laws.
• The bill seeks to replace imprisonment with monetary penalties for several petty offences, thereby reducing the burden on the judicial system.
• It covers amendments to numerous central acts, promoting ease of doing business and ease of living for citizens.
• The move is expected to decongest courts and jails, allowing the justice system to focus on more serious crimes.
• This legislative reform aligns with the government's objective of promoting trust-based governance.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: The Jan Vishwas Bill aims to amend multiple central acts.
Its primary objective is to convert imprisonment for minor offences into monetary penalties.
The bill is intended to promote 'ease of doing business' and 'ease of living'.
It seeks to reduce the caseload on the Indian judiciary.
The bill was first introduced in 2022 and underwent parliamentary scrutiny.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the Jan Vishwas (Amendment) Bill and its potential impact on the Indian legal and judicial system.
Discuss how decriminalization of minor offences can contribute to 'ease of doing business' and 'ease of living' in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of replacing imprisonment with monetary penalties as a deterrent for minor violations.
Examine the challenges in implementing such a broad legislative reform across various central acts.
Suggest further reforms needed to decongest courts and promote a more efficient justice delivery system in India.
GS2.imageDescription: The Parliament House of India, symbolizing legislative action and reform.
GS2.trivia: The concept of 'decriminalization' has been gaining traction globally as a way to modernize legal frameworks and reduce the social and economic costs associated with minor infractions.

GS2.headline: Health Ministry Rolls Out Nationwide Program to Combat Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in Rural Areas
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched a comprehensive nationwide program targeting the prevention and control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in rural India.
• The initiative focuses on early screening for common NCDs like diabetes, hypertension, and certain cancers at primary healthcare centers.
• It emphasizes lifestyle modifications, awareness campaigns, and providing affordable access to diagnostics and treatment in underserved areas.
• The program leverages digital health platforms and community health workers (ASHAs) for outreach and follow-up.
• This expansion aims to reduce the growing burden of NCDs, which are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in India.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NCDs include conditions such as heart disease, stroke, cancer, diabetes, and chronic respiratory diseases.
The National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS) is a major government initiative against NCDs.
ASHAs play a crucial role in delivering healthcare services at the community level in rural India.
Lifestyle factors like unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, tobacco use, and harmful alcohol consumption are major risk factors for NCDs.
The program aims to strengthen the healthcare infrastructure in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India, particularly in rural areas, and its socio-economic implications.
Analyze the key features of the new nationwide program to combat NCDs in rural India and its potential effectiveness.
Evaluate the role of primary healthcare centers, community health workers, and digital platforms in the successful implementation of such a program.
Examine the challenges in delivering comprehensive NCD care and promoting preventive healthcare in remote and underserved regions.
Suggest policy recommendations to strengthen India's public health infrastructure and address the NCD crisis effectively.
GS2.imageDescription: A community health worker (ASHA) interacting with rural villagers, providing health advice and screening services.
GS2.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths. Over three quarters of NCD deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries.

GS2.headline: India Pledges Strong Support for Global South at G77 + China Summit
GS2.explainer:
• India reaffirmed its commitment to championing the interests of the Global South at the G77 + China Summit, advocating for a more equitable and inclusive global order.
• The External Affairs Minister highlighted India's role in addressing challenges faced by developing nations, including climate change, debt distress, and food security.
• India called for reforms in international financial institutions and multilateral bodies to better represent the voices of the Global South.
• Discussions focused on fostering greater cooperation in areas of sustainable development, technology transfer, and capacity building.
• This stance underscores India's growing leadership and influence as a voice for developing countries on the global stage.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora - their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The Group of 77 (G77) at the United Nations is a coalition of developing nations, designed to promote its members' collective economic interests.
It was founded in 1964 by 77 developing countries; it now has 134 member countries.
China, while not a formal member of the G77, cooperates with the group and often issues joint statements.
India is a prominent member of the G77.
Key issues for the Global South include climate finance, debt sustainability, and equitable trade.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's historical and contemporary role as a leader and advocate for the Global South in international forums.
Analyze the major challenges faced by the Global South and how India's foreign policy addresses these concerns.
Evaluate the effectiveness of groupings like G77 + China in influencing global governance and promoting the interests of developing countries.
Examine the call for reforms in international financial institutions and multilateral bodies from the perspective of the Global South.
Suggest how India can further strengthen its leadership position and foster greater unity among Global South nations.
GS2.imageDescription: A world map highlighting countries of the Global South, with India prominently featured, symbolizing its leadership.
GS2.trivia: The first meeting of the G77 was held in Algiers, Algeria, in 1967, where they adopted the 'Charter of Algiers', outlining their common position on international economic issues.

GS2.headline: Election Commission Announces Schedule for Upcoming State Assembly Elections
GS2.explainer:
• The Election Commission of India (ECI) announced the detailed schedule for upcoming Assembly elections in three states: Jharkhand, Jammu & Kashmir, and Maharashtra.
• The polls are slated to be held in multiple phases over the next two months, with strict adherence to electoral guidelines and security protocols.
• The announcement triggers the Model Code of Conduct, prohibiting governments from announcing new schemes or policies that could influence voters.
• Extensive preparations are underway, including deployment of security forces, voter awareness campaigns, and arrangements for smooth conduct of elections.
• These elections are crucial for the political landscape and will set the tone for national politics ahead of the next general elections.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
prelimsPoints: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.
Article 324 of the Constitution provides for an Election Commission.
The Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the ECI for political parties and candidates during elections.
Jammu & Kashmir has been under central rule since 2018, and these elections would mark a significant return to democratic processes.
The Chief Election Commissioner and Election Commissioners are appointed by the President of India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of the Election Commission of India in upholding free and fair elections and strengthening democratic processes.
Analyze the significance of state assembly elections, particularly in states like Jammu & Kashmir, for national politics and governance.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the ECI in conducting elections, including maintaining law and order, ensuring voter participation, and enforcing the Model Code of Conduct.
Examine the impact of electoral reforms and technological advancements on the conduct of elections in India.
Suggest measures to further enhance the transparency, accessibility, and integrity of the Indian electoral system.
GS2.imageDescription: The emblem of the Election Commission of India with ballot boxes, symbolizing the democratic process.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952.

GS2.headline: India's Digital Public Infrastructure Model Gains Global Recognition at UN Forum
GS2.explainer:
• India's pioneering Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model, encompassing Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker, received widespread acclaim at a recent UN forum.
• Delegations from several developing nations expressed keen interest in adopting and adapting India's DPI framework to accelerate their own digital transformation.
• India showcased how DPI has enabled financial inclusion, efficient public service delivery, and fostered innovation at scale.
• The UN forum highlighted DPI as a critical enabler for achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and bridging the digital divide.
• India pledged to support other countries in building their own DPI, emphasizing a collaborative approach to global digital development.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora - their structure, mandate.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) refers to foundational digital systems that are available to all, enabling a wide range of public and private services.
Aadhaar is India's unique identification number based on biometric and demographic data.
Unified Payments Interface (UPI) is an instant real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
DigiLocker is a cloud-based platform for issuance and verification of documents and certificates.
DPI is seen as a crucial tool for achieving various Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
mainsPoints: Discuss the components and functionalities of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model and its transformative impact on governance and economy.
Analyze why India's DPI model is gaining global recognition and its potential for replication in other developing countries.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities associated with promoting an open, inclusive, and interoperable DPI framework globally.
Examine how DPI contributes to achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and fostering financial inclusion.
Suggest strategies for India to further strengthen its DPI and lead global efforts in digital public goods development.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized representation of interconnected digital networks with icons of Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker, symbolizing India's DPI.
GS2.trivia: India's UPI system processed over 10 billion transactions in a single month in 2023, making it one of the world's most successful digital payment platforms.

GS2.headline: India Assumes Chairmanship of Indian Ocean Rim Association (IORA)
GS2.explainer:
• India officially assumed the chairmanship of the Indian Ocean Rim Association (IORA) for a two-year term, marking a significant milestone in its regional diplomacy.
• As chair, India pledged to prioritize maritime security, blue economy development, regional trade facilitation, and cultural exchange among member states.
• The External Affairs Minister outlined India's vision for a secure, prosperous, and sustainable Indian Ocean region, emphasizing inclusivity and cooperation.
• This leadership role provides India with a crucial platform to shape the regional agenda and strengthen its strategic influence.
• IORA members lauded India's proactive approach and its commitment to fostering greater collaboration in the Indian Ocean.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora - their structure, mandate.
India and its neighborhood- relations.
prelimsPoints: IORA is an inter-governmental organization established in 1997.
It aims to strengthen regional cooperation and sustainable development within the Indian Ocean region.
IORA has 23 member states and 10 dialogue partners.
India is a founding member of IORA.
Key priority areas for IORA include maritime safety and security, trade and investment facilitation, fisheries management, disaster risk management, and cultural and tourism cooperation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of India assuming the chairmanship of IORA and its implications for India's role in the Indian Ocean region.
Analyze the key priorities India is likely to pursue during its IORA chairmanship, focusing on maritime security, blue economy, and regional trade.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for fostering greater cooperation among IORA member states in the context of evolving geopolitical dynamics.
Examine how IORA contributes to India's broader 'SAGAR' (Security and Growth for All in the Region) vision.
Suggest concrete steps India can take to enhance IORA's effectiveness and address common regional challenges.
GS2.imageDescription: A map of the Indian Ocean region with flags of IORA member countries, highlighting India's central position.
GS2.trivia: The concept of IORA originated during a visit by former South African President Nelson Mandela to India in 1995, where he spoke of the vision for an 'Indian Ocean Rim for regional cooperation'.

GS3.headline: India's Manufacturing PMI Hits 18-Month High Amid Strong Domestic Demand
GS3.explainer:
• The S&P Global India Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) rose to its highest level in 18 months in December 2025.
• This surge is attributed to robust domestic demand, increased new orders, and improved production levels across various sectors.
• The strong performance indicates a sustained recovery in the manufacturing sector, contributing significantly to economic growth.
• Employment levels also saw a marginal increase, suggesting positive sentiment among manufacturers.
• Experts predict continued momentum into the new year, supported by government infrastructure spending and festive season consumption.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Manufacturing sector performance and challenges.
prelimsPoints: PMI is a survey-based economic indicator derived from monthly surveys of private sector companies.
A PMI reading above 50 indicates expansion, while a reading below 50 indicates contraction.
It covers sectors like manufacturing and services.
S&P Global compiles the PMI data for India.
New orders and output are key sub-components of the PMI.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the recent surge in India's manufacturing PMI and its implications for overall economic growth.
Discuss the challenges that continue to hinder the manufacturing sector's full potential in India, despite positive indicators.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies, such as 'Make in India' and PLI schemes, in boosting manufacturing output and employment.
Examine the correlation between domestic demand, exports, and manufacturing sector performance.
Suggest policy measures to sustain and further accelerate growth in India's manufacturing sector.
GS3.imageDescription: A factory floor with machinery in operation, symbolizing industrial growth and manufacturing activity.
GS3.trivia: The first PMI surveys were conducted in the United States in 1931 by the National Association of Purchasing Management (NAPM), now known as the Institute for Supply Management (ISM).

GS3.headline: Government Launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) Phase II
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare has officially launched the second phase of the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA).
• Phase II focuses on scaling up climate-smart agricultural practices, promoting drought-resistant crop varieties, and enhancing water-use efficiency across vulnerable regions.
• The mission aims to build the adaptive capacity of Indian farmers to climate change impacts, ensuring food security and sustainable livelihoods.
• Key initiatives include extensive farmer training programs, deployment of advanced weather forecasting tools, and promotion of agro-forestry.
• A substantial financial outlay has been approved to support research, infrastructure development, and technology adoption in climate-resilient farming.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Agriculture: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Food processing and related industries in India— scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: NMCRA is a key initiative under India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).
NAPCC was launched in 2008 and comprises eight national missions.
Climate-smart agriculture (CSA) integrates food security and climate change goals.
Key components of CSA include sustainable land and water management, crop diversification, and improved livestock management.
The mission aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from agriculture while adapting to climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical need for climate-resilient agriculture in India, considering the country's vulnerability to climate change impacts.
Analyze the key features and expected outcomes of NMCRA Phase II, particularly its focus on scaling up practices and technology adoption.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing climate-resilient agricultural practices across diverse agro-climatic zones in India.
Examine how NMCRA contributes to India's commitments under international climate agreements and national food security goals.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy interventions to further strengthen climate resilience in the Indian agricultural sector.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer working in a field with modern irrigation systems, demonstrating climate-resilient agricultural practices.
GS3.trivia: The National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) was launched to address climate change by outlining eight core missions, including the National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA), which NMCRA is a part of.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful test flight of its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator.
• This critical test involved a controlled landing experiment, simulating the return of a spacecraft from orbit, marking a significant step towards India's future space capabilities.
• The RLV aims to drastically reduce the cost of space launches by enabling multiple uses of the launch vehicle, similar to an aircraft.
• The mission incorporated advanced autonomous navigation, guidance, and control systems for precise landing.
• This achievement positions India among a select few nations developing such cutting-edge space technologies.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO's RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) project is a series of experiments to master key technologies.
The RLV-TD program includes the Hypersonic Flight Experiment (HEX), Landing Experiment (LEX), Return Flight Experiment (REX), and Scramjet Propulsion Experiment (SPEX).
The current test likely relates to the Landing Experiment (LEX) phase.
Reusable launch vehicles aim to reduce launch costs by reusing expensive components.
Other countries like the USA (SpaceX, NASA) and China are also developing RLV technologies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic and economic significance of developing Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space program.
Analyze how RLV technology can revolutionize space access, making it more affordable and frequent.
Examine the challenges and complexities involved in designing and implementing RLV systems.
Evaluate India's position in the global race for advanced space technologies and the role of ISRO's RLV program.
Suggest future directions for India's space exploration efforts, leveraging RLV capabilities.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) returning to Earth, with smoke trails and landing gear deployed.
GS3.trivia: The first successful reusable space vehicle was the Space Shuttle program by NASA, which operated from 1981 to 2011.

GS3.headline: RBI Governor Cautions Against Global Economic Headwinds, Maintains Policy Stance
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Governor reiterated concerns about persistent global economic headwinds, including geopolitical tensions and commodity price volatility.
• Despite these challenges, the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to maintain the existing policy repo rate, citing inflation management and growth support as key priorities.
• The Governor emphasized the need for continued vigilance and data-driven policy calibration in the face of evolving global and domestic economic conditions.
• Projections for inflation and GDP growth were reviewed, with a cautious optimism for India's economic resilience.
• The RBI affirmed its commitment to ensuring financial stability and anchoring inflation expectations.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary Policy and its impact on economy.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate in India.
The Governor of the RBI is the ex-officio Chairperson of the MPC.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Global economic headwinds include factors like geopolitical conflicts, supply chain disruptions, and energy price fluctuations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth amidst global economic uncertainties.
Discuss the effectiveness of the RBI's current monetary policy stance in addressing both domestic inflation and external shocks.
Evaluate the impact of global economic headwinds, such as geopolitical tensions and commodity price volatility, on India's economic stability.
Examine the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in formulating and implementing monetary policy decisions.
Suggest potential policy options for the RBI to safeguard India's economy against future global downturns.
GS3.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai, symbolizing the central bank's authority and role in monetary policy.
GS3.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the provisions of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: Joint Naval Exercise 'Varuna' Concludes Between India and France
GS3.explainer:
• The latest edition of the bilateral naval exercise 'Varuna' between the Indian Navy and the French Navy successfully concluded in the Arabian Sea.
• The exercise involved advanced air defence drills, anti-submarine warfare operations, tactical maneuvers, and cross-deck landings.
• 'Varuna' aims to enhance interoperability, share best practices, and strengthen maritime cooperation between the two navies.
• The drills underscored the strategic partnership between India and France in ensuring maritime security and stability in the Indo-Pacific region.
• Both nations reaffirmed their commitment to a rules-based international order and freedom of navigation.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: 'Varuna' is a bilateral naval exercise between India and France.
It is one of the key defence exercises regularly conducted by India with friendly nations.
The exercise typically involves aircraft carriers, destroyers, frigates, submarines, and maritime patrol aircraft.
India and France share a strategic partnership, particularly in defence and space.
The exercise often takes place in the Arabian Sea or the Indian Ocean region.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of joint naval exercises like 'Varuna' in strengthening India's strategic partnerships and enhancing maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Analyze the evolving defence cooperation between India and France and its implications for regional stability.
Examine how such exercises contribute to improving interoperability and sharing best practices between navies.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in building a strong network of defence partnerships in the current geopolitical scenario.
Suggest ways to deepen India-France defence and strategic ties beyond naval exercises.
GS3.imageDescription: Warships of the Indian and French navies sailing in formation during Exercise Varuna, with a helicopter taking off from a deck.
GS3.trivia: The 'Varuna' exercise began in 1993 and was christened as 'Varuna' in 2001, becoming a hallmark of India-France strategic naval cooperation.

GS3.headline: India's First Quantum Computing Lab Inaugurated in Bengaluru
GS3.explainer:
• India achieved a significant milestone in advanced technology with the inauguration of its first dedicated Quantum Computing Lab in Bengaluru.
• The state-of-the-art facility will serve as a hub for research, development, and innovation in quantum algorithms, quantum cryptography, and quantum communication.
• The lab, a joint initiative between government, academia, and private industry, aims to accelerate India's capabilities in this strategic domain.
• It is expected to foster a new generation of quantum scientists and engineers, contributing to the National Quantum Mission.
• This development positions India as a key player in the global race for quantum supremacy, with potential applications in defence, healthcare, and finance.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Quantum computing uses principles of quantum mechanics (superposition, entanglement) to process information.
Unlike classical computers that use bits, quantum computers use qubits.
Potential applications include drug discovery, materials science, financial modeling, and breaking current encryption methods.
The National Quantum Mission (NQM) was approved by the Union Cabinet in 2023.
Bengaluru is often referred to as the 'Silicon Valley of India'.
mainsPoints: Discuss the revolutionary potential of quantum computing and its implications for India's technological advancement and strategic autonomy.
Analyze the objectives and expected outcomes of establishing India's first dedicated Quantum Computing Lab in line with the National Quantum Mission.
Examine the challenges and ethical considerations associated with the development and deployment of quantum technologies.
Evaluate India's current position and future prospects in the global race for quantum supremacy.
Suggest policy measures and investment strategies to accelerate research, talent development, and industrial application of quantum computing in India.
GS3.imageDescription: An abstract depiction of a quantum processor, with glowing lines and interconnected nodes, representing complex quantum computations.
GS3.trivia: The term 'quantum computing' was first coined by physicist Paul Benioff in 1980.

GS3.headline: FDI Inflows Show Robust Growth in Q3, Driven by Green Energy and Tech Sectors
GS3.explainer:
• India witnessed robust growth in Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) inflows during the third quarter of 2025-26, indicating continued investor confidence in the economy.
• A significant portion of these investments was directed towards the burgeoning green energy sector, including renewable energy projects and electric vehicle manufacturing.
• The technology sector, particularly IT services and digital infrastructure, also attracted substantial FDI, reflecting India's growing digital economy.
• Government policy reforms, such as ease of doing business initiatives and production-linked incentive (PLI) schemes, are credited for creating an attractive investment climate.
• This surge in FDI is expected to boost employment, foster technological advancements, and contribute to India's long-term economic development.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Investment models.
prelimsPoints: FDI is an investment made by a firm or individual in one country into business interests located in another country.
FDI is a major non-debt creating source of financing for economic development.
The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) is responsible for FDI policy and data.
Green energy includes solar, wind, hydro, and other renewable energy sources.
PLI schemes offer incentives to companies for incremental sales from products manufactured in domestic units.
mainsPoints: Analyze the recent trends in India's FDI inflows, highlighting the sectors that are attracting significant investment and the reasons behind it.
Discuss the role of government policies, such as 'ease of doing business' reforms and PLI schemes, in promoting FDI in key sectors.
Evaluate the economic benefits of increased FDI, including its impact on employment, technology transfer, and export competitiveness.
Examine the challenges and concerns associated with attracting and sustaining large-scale FDI in India.
Suggest further policy measures to enhance India's attractiveness as an investment destination, particularly in emerging sectors.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing upward arrows and currency symbols, representing robust growth in foreign direct investment.
GS3.trivia: Singapore and Mauritius are historically among the top source countries for FDI into India.

GS3.headline: Gujarat Announces New Solar Power Policy to Boost Renewable Energy Capacity
GS3.explainer:
• The Gujarat government unveiled its ambitious new Solar Power Policy, aiming to significantly increase the state's renewable energy capacity.
• The policy introduces enhanced incentives for residential, commercial, and industrial consumers to adopt rooftop solar installations.
• It simplifies the regulatory framework for setting up solar projects and promotes grid integration of decentralized solar generation.
• The government expects the new policy to attract substantial private investment in the solar sector and generate employment opportunities.
• This move aligns with India's national goal of achieving 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Gujarat is one of the leading states in India for renewable energy generation.
Rooftop solar refers to solar panels installed on the roofs of buildings.
Net metering is a billing mechanism that credits solar energy system owners for the electricity they add to the grid.
India aims to achieve 'Panchamrit' goals, including 500 GW non-fossil energy capacity by 2030.
The International Solar Alliance (ISA) is an initiative launched by India and France.
mainsPoints: Discuss the key features and objectives of Gujarat's new Solar Power Policy and its potential impact on the state's renewable energy landscape.
Analyze how state-level policies can contribute to India's national targets for renewable energy capacity addition and climate change mitigation.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in promoting decentralized solar generation, particularly rooftop solar, in urban and rural areas.
Examine the role of incentives, regulatory frameworks, and public awareness in accelerating the adoption of solar energy.
Suggest best practices for other states to emulate in developing effective renewable energy policies.
GS3.imageDescription: Solar panels on a rooftop, with a bright sun in the background, symbolizing clean energy generation.
GS3.trivia: India's first solar-powered railway station is located in Guwahati, Assam.

GS3.headline: Government Designates New Conservation Reserve in Western Ghats for Endangered Species Protection
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced the establishment of a new Conservation Reserve within the ecologically sensitive Western Ghats region.
• This protected area is specifically designated to safeguard critically endangered species, including a unique amphibian species and several endemic plant varieties.
• The move aims to strengthen biodiversity conservation efforts and mitigate human-wildlife conflict in the region.
• The reserve will implement strict conservation protocols, community engagement programs, and scientific research initiatives.
• Environmentalists have lauded the decision as a crucial step towards preserving the rich biodiversity of the Western Ghats, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
prelimsPoints: Conservation Reserves and Community Reserves are protected areas in India governed by the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
Conservation Reserves are intermediate protected areas between National Parks/Wildlife Sanctuaries and reserved/protected forests.
The Western Ghats is one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.
It is recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Many species found in the Western Ghats are endemic, meaning they are found nowhere else in the world.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Western Ghats as a biodiversity hotspot and the critical need for designating new conservation reserves.
Analyze the features and objectives of Conservation Reserves as a tool for biodiversity protection in India.
Evaluate the challenges involved in managing protected areas, including human-wildlife conflict, encroachment, and funding constraints.
Examine how community engagement and sustainable livelihood initiatives can contribute to the success of conservation efforts.
Suggest a comprehensive strategy for integrating biodiversity conservation with regional development in ecologically sensitive areas like the Western Ghats.
GS3.imageDescription: Lush green landscape of the Western Ghats with a waterfall and diverse flora, symbolizing its rich biodiversity.
GS3.trivia: The Western Ghats are older than the Himalayan mountain range. They influence Indian monsoon weather patterns by intercepting the moisture-laden monsoon winds.

sources: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
